VAL-VIDEO-003.evidence: Navigated to ReportPage for audit e2ed0938-ca82-4e84-b76d-ec788e860c51. The 'Session Recordings' h2 heading IS present and correctly positioned after 'Screenshot Timeline' (index 4 vs index 3 in DOM order). A video-grid with 4 video-card elements is rendered, each with correct scenario-persona labels: Cookie Consent/Privacy Sensitive, Cookie Consent/Cost Sensitive, Checkout Flow/Privacy Sensitive, Checkout Flow/Cost Sensitive. HOWEVER, there are ZERO <video> elements in the DOM (document.querySelectorAll('video').length === 0, document.querySelectorAll('video[controls]').length === 0). All 4 video-player-container divs contain a video-error div with '📹 Video unavailable' text instead of <video> elements. The onError handler in the video component replaces the <video> element with this error state because the mock .webm placeholder files (37 bytes) are not valid WebM containers that browsers can decode.
VAL-VIDEO-003.details: The assertion requires 'an HTML5

blockers.description: Mock .webm placeholder files are 37 bytes (invalid WebM format). Browsers cannot decode them, triggering the HTML5 media error event. The video component's onError handler replaces <video> elements with 'Video unavailable' error divs. This means the <video controls> elements required by the assertion are never visible in the DOM at test time.
blockers.quickFixAttempted: None - this is a known issue from round 1. The mock video files need to be valid WebM containers (even minimal ones) for the <video> element to remain in the DOM with controls attribute.

summary: Tested 1 assertion: 0 passed, 1 failed. VAL-VIDEO-003 FAILS because the 4 video cards all show 'Video unavailable' error state instead of <video controls> elements. The Session Recordings section structure (heading, grid, labeled cards) is correct, but invalid 37-byte mock .webm files cause browser decode errors, triggering the error handler that removes <video> elements from the DOM.
